content: The Linux kernel boot process begins with the architecture-specific entry point in arch/x86/boot/header.S. The boot protocol starts in real mode, transitions to protected mode via go_to_protected_mode(), then jumps to the 32-bit startup_32 in arch/x86/boot/compressed/head_64.S. After decompressing the kernel image, control transfers to start_kernel() in init/main.c, which initializes all core subsystems sequentially.

start_kernel() in init/main.c is the first C function executed during boot. It calls setup_arch() for architecture-specific initialization, mm_init() to set up the memory management subsystem, sched_init() for the scheduler, and init_IRQ() for interrupt handling. The function ends by calling arch_call_rest_init(), which spawns kernel_init as PID 1 and kthreadd as PID 2.

The Linux kernel uses the initcall mechanism to initialize subsystems and drivers in a defined order. Initcalls are grouped into levels: early_initcall, core_initcall, postcore_initcall, arch_initcall, subsys_initcall, fs_initcall, device_initcall, and late_initcall. Each level is invoked sequentially by do_initcalls() in init/main.c. Drivers register themselves using module_init() which maps to device_initcall level.

The Linux kernel memory management subsystem provides virtual memory, page allocation, slab caching, and memory-mapped I/O. The buddy allocator in mm/page_alloc.c manages physical pages in power-of-two blocks. The slab allocator (SLUB) in mm/slub.c provides efficient allocation of small, frequently-used kernel objects. Virtual memory is managed through multi-level page tables with architecture-specific implementations.

The OOM (Out-Of-Memory) killer is triggered when the system runs out of memory and cannot reclaim enough pages. The OOM killer selects a victim process based on oom_score calculated from RSS, oom_score_adj, and other factors. The function out_of_memory() in mm/oom_kill.c orchestrates the selection via select_bad_process(). Processes can be protected from OOM killing by setting /proc/PID/oom_score_adj to -1000.

U-Boot (Universal Boot Loader) initialization follows a two-stage process. The first stage (SPL/TPL) runs from SRAM, initializes DRAM via board_init_f(), and relocates U-Boot to DRAM. The second stage runs board_init_r() from DRAM, initializing all subsystems including serial console, network, storage, and the command-line interface. The entry point is _start in arch/arm/cpu/armv7/start.S.

U-Boot uses an init_sequence mechanism to execute initialization steps in order. board_init_f() iterates over init_sequence_f[] array, calling each function pointer sequentially. Key steps include setup_mon_len (monitor size), fdtdec_setup (device tree), init_baud_rate (serial baud), and dram_init (DRAM sizing). If any init function returns non-zero, the boot process halts. The init sequence is defined in common/board_f.c.

The Linux device driver model is built around three core abstractions: bus, device, and driver, implemented in drivers/base/. When a new device is registered via device_register(), the bus iterates over all registered drivers calling match() to find a compatible driver. When a match is found, the driver's probe() function is called. The sysfs filesystem in /sys/ exposes the device hierarchy to userspace.

Platform devices in Linux use the platform_bus for SoC-integrated peripherals that are not discoverable via hardware enumeration. Device information comes from Device Tree (DT) blobs parsed at boot time. The platform_driver structure contains probe() and remove() callbacks. Matching between device and driver uses the compatible string in the of_match_table. The DT binding is processed by of_platform_populate() which creates platform_device instances.
